title: 18" Kitchen Cabinet Drawer Base
summary: An 18"-wide base cabinet built around three stacked drawers, with mirrored side panels, a separately built face frame, and full-overlay drawer faces.
description:
The two side panels are cut and drilled as true mirror images of each other, which matters more here than on a single-door cabinet: pocket holes go on the OUTSIDE of both panels, and it's genuinely easy to drill one panel backward if you're not deliberately checking which side is which before you drill. Getting the mirroring right is what lets the face frame hide every pocket hole on the finished cabinet.

The two drawer openings are sized slightly differently on purpose — not a mistake to fix, but what makes the drawer FACES come out identical, with the face frame's bottom rail centered between them rather than the openings themselves. This is a small detail that's easy to "correct" into a worse-looking cabinet if you don't know it's intentional.

tools.note: Cuts the toe-kick notches in the side panels.
Rips the plywood into the various strip widths this cabinet needs.

materials.name: Plywood, 3/4", 4x8 sheet
materials.note: Ripped into 22-1/4" strips for sides/shelf, 5" strips for base supports, and scrap for top supports.

materials.name: Drawer slide sets, 21-1/2" to 22-1/4" interior length
Drawer/door face material, 3/4" plywood or MDF
materials.note: Cut into one small and two large full-overlay faces.

cutList.note: Cut as true mirror images; pocket holes go on the outside of both.

steps.title: Cut all the parts
steps.body: Break the 3/4" sheet down before anything else. Rip two 22-1/4" strips off the 48" width — that width sets a finished 24" cabinet depth once the face frame and drawer faces are on, and every cabinet in a run has to match it. From the first strip crosscut the two side panels at 34-1/2" and the bottom shelf at 16"; from the second strip rip a 5" band for the two bottom supports at 16", and take the two 3-1/2" top supports at 16" from the 3-1/2" strip left over after the two 22-1/4" rips. Cut the 1/4" back to 17-1/2" x 32". From the 1x2, cut two face frame stiles at 27" and four rails to your chosen frame layout (see the face-frame step). From the 3/4" face material cut one small drawer face at 5-1/2" x 17-1/2" and two large ones at 11" x 17-1/2". A single 4x8 sheet covers all the 3/4" plywood parts with plenty left over.
materials: Drawer/door face material, 3/4" plywood or MDF

steps.title: Cut the toe kick and drill the side panels
steps.body: Cut the toe kick notch into the bottom front corner of each 22-1/4" x 34-1/2" side panel with a jigsaw; make a pattern block from scrap and trace it onto both panels so the two notches are identical, which matters because the cabinet stands on them. Then drill 3/4" pocket holes along the OUTSIDE face of both panels, in from each long edge where the bottom shelf, base supports and top supports will land. Lay the two panels side by side, mark which is left and which is right, and confirm they are true mirror images before the drill touches either — the pocket holes go on the outside of both so the face frame can hide them, and it is genuinely easy to drill the second panel as a duplicate instead of a mirror.

steps.title: Attach the base supports
steps.body: Lay one side panel flat, pocket holes facing up and away from you, and stand the two 5" x 16" base supports on their edges across it — one flush with the front edge above the toe kick, one at the back edge. Because they are 16" long and land between the two 3/4" panels, the carcass will finish 17-1/2" wide overall. Screw through the side panel's pocket holes into each support with 1-1/4" pocket hole screws, keeping the supports square to the panel edge.

steps.title: Attach the bottom shelf
steps.body: Set the 22-1/4" x 16" bottom shelf on top of the two base supports, its front edge flush with the front support and its side edge tight to the side panel. Screw down into both supports and across into the side panel with 1-1/4" pocket hole screws. The 16" shelf and the 16" supports are the same length on purpose — they set the cabinet's inside clear width, so if the shelf ends up proud of a support, fix it now rather than after the second side goes on.

steps.title: Close the carcass
steps.body: Lower the second side panel onto the assembly, pocket holes again facing outward, and drive 1-1/4" pocket hole screws into the ends of both base supports and the bottom shelf. Add the two 3-1/2" x 16" top supports across the top, one at the front and one at the back, which is what holds the box to width up there and gives the countertop something to screw into later. Measure both diagonals across the open front before the last screws go in — 17-1/2" of clear box width at the bottom and something different at the top means the drawers will bind.

steps.title: Attach the back
steps.body: Add the back panel with glue and finish nails or staples.

steps.title: Build and attach the face frame
steps.body: Build the face frame flat on a table before it goes anywhere near the cabinet, joining the two 27" stiles to the rails with 3/4" pocket holes, 1-1/4" screws and glue, and checking both diagonals as you clamp. The cut list is inconsistent about the rails — two are listed at 18" (full frame width, stiles between) and two at 15" (fitting between two 1-1/2" stiles) — so pick one convention and cut all four rails to it before you assemble; mixing the two gives you a frame that is neither 18" wide nor square. Size the upper drawer opening slightly larger than the lower one on purpose: that is what lets the two large drawer faces come out identical with the rail centered between them, and it is not an error to correct. Attach the finished frame to the cabinet front with pocket holes and glue, leaving about 1/4" of frame proud on each side of the 17-1/2" box.

steps.title: Build and install the drawers
steps.body: Measure the actual clear width inside your cabinet — it should be about 16" between the side panels — and build each drawer box narrower by the clearance your slides require, which for typical side-mount slides is about 1/2" per side, so roughly 15" wide. Build the boxes deep enough to suit the slides listed, whose 21-1/2" to 22-1/4" interior length matches the 22-1/4" carcass depth. Mount the slides level and at matching heights on both sides of each opening, measuring up from the bottom shelf rather than down from the top, then hang the boxes and check that each rolls without rubbing the face frame.
materials: Drawer slide sets, 21-1/2" to 22-1/4" interior length

steps.title: Attach the drawer faces
steps.body: The faces are full overlay at 17-1/2" wide — a 1/4" reveal against the 18" face frame on each side — with the small face 5-1/2" tall at the top and the two large faces 11" each below it. Working one drawer at a time, shim the face into position with 1/8" spacers, clamp it to the drawer box, and drive screws from INSIDE the box through the box front into the face so no fastener shows. Do the top face first and work down, checking the reveals with the same spacer each time rather than by eye.
materials: Drawer/door face material, 3/4" plywood or MDF

images.alt: 18-inch kitchen base cabinet with three stacked drawers
